segments.text: We've got, uh, Shane Marsh here, chief Executive
of dos, uh, online.
Welcome, Shane. You've got 15 minutes.
If you can give us some introductory remarks.
Thank you for your written submission.
And then we'll go to questions. So, good morning.
Good morning. Thank you,
uh, for having me along this morning.
Uh, so, uh, we've got really two, uh, start my video,
two key elements of the update from DOS in terms of
how we are working to, uh,
improve competition in the the banking sector.
Uh, the first is that, um, DOS believes that to add value
to Kiwis, uh, in banking services, the best approach is
to build an integrated ecosystem.
Over the last year, we've added, uh, Westpac Visa,
and most recently, one New Zealand, uh,
to a broader ecosystem of, uh, organizations
that are working together to provide innovative
financial services to Kiwis.
The most recent one, uh,
one New Zealand launched a couple weeks ago.
Uh, it really is an, an innovation, uh, in New Zealand.
Uh, together we are offering, um, new value to customers,
um, not only in terms
of the financial services they receive, such as, uh, uh,
great interest rates, innovative, uh, uh, value in terms
of their home loans, uh, but also their card spend,
but also enabling customers to get a faster access
to the leading technology in terms of mobile phone devices,
uh, very new in the market.
Um, there's a number of instances offshore where
telecommunications works
with fintechs and banking providers.
Um, and there's been a lot of success from that as well.
So what we're doing in New Zealand is really looking at
how we can bring the best, uh, solutions
that have worked offshore to New Zealand to add better value
to Kiwis in New Zealand.
Um, the second topic is really around an update in terms
of the application we made to the Reserve Bank, uh,
for an application for a a banking license.
Um, if we kind of step back for a bit, um, was, uh,
originated out of, uh, Singapore, where myself,
my co-founder, we were working in a market that had a lot
of competition and banking, new services, innovation.
And we've seen that around the world, uh, from the UK
with the likes of Monzo
and in Australia, with the likes of UP bank, really bringing
new digital services, innovation and competition
and value to those, uh, countries and markets.
Our approach has always been
to look at what's working offshore
and how do we bring that to New Zealand
to offer better value in a market that, to be honest, has,
has fallen behind, um, what we're seeing, um,
been offered around the world.
The model that has been most successful in other countries
has been, uh, in a transactional bank model.
So a transactional bank model is where a new entrant offers,
uh, accounts payments, uh, cards
and savings to customers generally through, uh,
a mobile app, um,
and provides better value, um, better customer experience.
Um, and through that, the likes of Monzo have been able
to scale rapidly, regularly, win, um, bank
of the Year awards, um, and then look
and at time as they scale
and mature to add additional products.
One thing that they're not offering is lending.
With lending comes lending, uh, greater regulatory, uh,
burden, uh, greater risk and greater capital requirements.
So it really is a, is a easy path, um, to,
to scale a bank.
And it's that model that we, uh, propose to the Reserve Bank
that dos would adopt, um, in New Zealand, uh, to get a,
a banking license, and then we would look
to develop the business from there.
Um, we relied on the definition of a bank,
uh, in the, um, banking credential Division Act, uh,
specifically, um, clause, uh, 73 1,
which says that a bank consists of the borrowing
and lending of money or the provision
of other financial services or both.
And it was our interpretation
that other financial services would include what we deemed
to be kind of basic banking services, the type that your,
uh, 18-year-old might take when they've getting their first
job, which is, I need an account, I need
to have my wages paid in, I want,
might wanna save some money.
Um, and I wanna make some,
some payments perhaps on a, on a card.
Um, the Reserve Bank has declined that model.
Um, the Reserve Bank deemed that in New Zealand, uh,
their interpretation of the legislation is that, uh,
lending is a mandatory requirement
for banking in New Zealand.
Um, that's a different position than what we see overseas.
Um, and we see that that is,
is a relatively narrow
and restrictive interpretation, um, of the legislation.
Um, and it means that, uh, the transactional model for,
for new entrant in New Zealand is not a path, um,
to becoming a bank here in this, um, this country.
We respect the Reserve Bank in terms
of their interpretation and expertise.
We've continued to have dialogue with them, um,
and we continue to consider whether, uh,
what our best future model is.
Um, we're open to resubmitting, um,
and if we do so, that would be, um,
by meeting the lending requirement.
Um, but from our perspective, um,
we have a flourishing business.
We're adding real value to Kiwis.
Uh, we're growing and scaling with our partners,
and so the best way forward is really depending on what's
the most viable, uh, in this market, um, albeit the pathways
that have been successful overseas are not available here.
Okay. Thank you. Thank you, Mr. Marsh.
We're gonna go to our deputy chair, Ryan Hamilton.
Uh, thanks Shane. Good to see you.
And, um, good to see the progress you're making in, in this,
um, innovative space.
Yeah, disappointed, obviously
to hear about your news on the Reserve Bank.
I understand we had them in last week.
They're still working on that bank definition stuff,
so hopefully there'll be the opportunity for you to reapply.
Um, you mentioned your submission,
otherwise you might have to narrow
your, your business model.
What would that, what would that look like?
Is that quite prohibitive for you?
Well, I think the main reason what we applied
to be a bank is that, uh, with, uh, the bank, you,
because you're regulated by the Reserve Bank
and you've got the bank title of bank in your name,
it brings a degree of trust
and credibility that, uh, consumers in New Zealand
can rely on so that they can have confidence
that they can place their money and take services.
So we've been able to build that up, um, without that,
but without doubt having that as, um, something we can stand
behind so that we can compete in the banking market,
we can talk about offering banking services, um, would be,
uh, an advantage for us.
So that's really the restriction is, um,
there's obviously some restriction around some
of the products we can offer in terms of lending
and how we offer them, but, uh, the question is really,
can you get to the point of competing with the likes
of the top five banks, um, without that, um,
that position in the market.
Thank you. And also, obviously, your relationship
with One New Zealand, I assume has been, uh, helped the work
of this committee, the work of the government on
differentiating prepaid interchange pricings for fintechs.
Can you maybe talk a bit about that
and how that's looking and shaping up for you?
Yeah, absolutely. So when, uh, the,
the government was looking at, uh, interchange pricing,
there was, uh, we had submitted that, uh,
for the prepaid option that we offer.
Uh, the interchange we use, uh, we generate is used
to add value to customers and to further grow our business.
And so that pricing was left, um, as it was.
What we've been able to do is to use that
to offer a really strong, um, opposition
and proposition the market together with one
that effectively enables, uh, anyone who spends anyone
who has a card to save up over time to,
to generate their next, uh, phone upgrade.
And as we know in this market, with the cost
of living crisis, it's really tough for people to be able
to, uh, afford effectively a luxury item,
but as we know, so critical to being, uh,
in the leading position in terms of tech and,
and access to the, the latest, um, devices.
So look, it's a real big positive.
Um, we're super excited to be working one nz.
They're a really customer focused organization,
and we think this has, uh, got great, um,
growth opportunities. Fantastic.
Okay. Thank you. Okay, thank you.
We're gonna go to, um, uh, Dr. Deborah Russell.
Yeah. Um, I'm just, uh, kind of a little bit puzzled.

Yeah, sorry. So we, um,

In itself.
'cause Westpac is doing the actual

So that's, that's all possible.
Um, but it's quite a lot of complexity

to be offering banking services.
Um, and the way in which, uh, we've, um,
approached it from a more of a transactional perspective.

Thanks Shane. Really

Daniel.

Policy

Wednesday 18 February 2026| Finance and Expenditure Committee
Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition
Dosh
Shane Marsh, Chief Executive
Kiwibank
Susan Peterson, Chair
Steve Jurkovich, Chief Executive
Non-bank Deposit Takers Association
Daniel McGrath, Xceda, CEO
John Healye, Wairarapa Building Society
